title: Creating Project and Personal Websites
text:
Quarto can generate full websites as well as single documents. This makes it useful for research groups, project pages, course materials, and personal academic profiles.


Web presence matters for:

public outreach
professional visibility
transparent sharing of outputs
communicating with collaborators, students, or stakeholders

Using Quarto for websites means the same writing habits you use in documents can also support online publishing.





Quarto websites use a project-level _quarto.yml file to define navigation and shared formatting.



The same Markdown, YAML, callouts, and code chunk patterns appear in websites too.



Project summaries, people pages, methods notes, and downloadable materials can all sit in one structured site.




A collaborative research project could maintain a site with:

a short overview
team members
methods documentation
workshop materials
links to publications and datasets







TipOutreach benefit



For many researchers, a small, well-maintained website is more useful than an ambitious site that never gets updated.





The exercise page focuses on planning and creating a small research website structure.
Go to the websites exercise

title: Writing Manuscripts
text:
Quarto can support manuscript writing by keeping narrative text, citations, figures, tables, and optional code in a single reproducible workflow. It is especially helpful when the same article needs to move between collaborator-friendly and submission-ready formats.


Manuscripts often require repeated changes across many components:

main text
references
tables and figures
supplementary material
multiple export formats

Quarto reduces friction by keeping these connected rather than scattered across separate systems.





The same source can often render to HTML, PDF, and DOCX, which is useful for review, sharing, and submission preparation.



Quarto works with bibliographies and citation styles, allowing references to stay embedded in the writing workflow.



When analysis is embedded, manuscript results become easier to update and verify.




A research team drafting a journal article might use HTML for internal review, DOCX for collaborators who prefer tracked changes, and PDF for a polished reading version. The content stays aligned because it comes from the same source file.






TipPractical starting point



You do not need to begin with a fully automated manuscript. Even using Quarto first as a well-structured writing environment already creates value.





The exercise page focuses on setting up a starter manuscript with metadata, sections, and room for references.
Go to the manuscripts exercise

title: Interactive Dashboards
text:
Quarto can also support interactive outputs, including dashboard-style communication. For researchers, this can be a useful bridge between formal publication and exploratory sharing with collaborators or stakeholders.


Some audiences need more than a static paper or slide deck. They may benefit from:

filterable summaries
updated visuals
browsable indicators
lightweight interaction around results

Quarto dashboards can serve these needs while staying close to the same publishing ecosystem.





Dashboards can organize findings into cards, sections, and panels that are easier to scan than a long narrative report.



Interactive views can still draw on the same documented analysis pipeline used elsewhere in a project.



Researchers do not need to begin with a complex application. Even a small dashboard with a few indicators can be valuable.




A project team monitoring restoration sites could create a dashboard summarizing site status, recent measurements, and key indicators for partners who do not need the full technical report.






WarningScope reminder



Dashboards can become technically demanding quickly. For a beginner workshop, it is often better to teach the concept and a simple starter pattern rather than a full production system.





The exercise page introduces a small dashboard concept suitable for first-time users.
Go to the dashboards exercise

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Quarto is an open-source scientific and technical publishing system created by Posit. It helps researchers move from scattered workflows to a more integrated way of writing, documenting, presenting, and sharing their work.
This workshop website is designed as a guided reference. You can follow it from top to bottom during a live session, or return to individual pages later when you need help with a specific output format.






NoteWorkshop idea



The core promise of Quarto is simple: write once in a plain text source file and render to multiple professional outputs such as HTML, PDF, DOCX, and slides.




This material is especially useful for:

early career researchers building their first durable workflow
experienced researchers who want fewer disconnected tools
teams that care about reproducibility, transparency, and reuse
anyone who wants websites, manuscripts, presentations, and notes to feel part of the same system

Basic familiarity with at least one language such as R, Python, or Julia can help, but it is not required for most of the lessons here.



By working through this site, participants will learn how to:

understand what Quarto is and where it fits relative to R Markdown
write basic content using Markdown and YAML
install Quarto and understand the main tooling options
use Quarto for lab notebooks, manuscripts, presentations, websites, and dashboards
explore more advanced tools such as extensions, templates, and brand.yml




The workshop is organized in three parts:

Start with the Foundations pages if Quarto is new to you.
Move into the Academic Use Cases to see how one tool supports different research outputs.
Finish with the Advanced Topics when you are ready to customize or scale your work.

Each use-case lesson has a separate guided exercise page. The lesson explains the workflow, and the exercise helps you practice it with an academic scenario.



Academic work usually produces many related outputs:

rough notes and exploratory documentation
figures and tables for papers
slides for talks
websites for projects, labs, or personal profiles
interactive communication for stakeholders or collaborators

Quarto makes these outputs easier to manage because the underlying authoring model stays consistent. That means less time relearning tools and more time focusing on the research itself.






TipSuggested path



If you are brand new, begin with What Is Quarto? and then continue to Markdown for Beginners.

title: One Tool to Rule Them All
text:
Academic work usually produces many related outputs:

rough notes and exploratory documentation
figures and tables for papers
slides for talks
websites for projects, labs, or personal profiles
interactive communication for stakeholders or collaborators

Quarto makes these outputs easier to manage because the underlying authoring model stays consistent. That means less time relearning tools and more time focusing on the research itself.






TipSuggested path



If you are brand new, begin with What Is Quarto? and then continue to Markdown for Beginners.

title: What Is Quarto?
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system developed by Posit. It is built for technical and scientific communication, which makes it especially useful for research settings where writing, analysis, and presentation often belong together.
Quarto is closely related to R Markdown, and many researchers meet it as the newer, broader continuation of ideas that became popular in the R Markdown ecosystem. The biggest shift is that Quarto was designed from the start as a multi-language, multi-format publishing system rather than as a tool mainly associated with R.


Research outputs rarely stay in one format. The same project may need:

notes during exploration
a manuscript draft
conference slides
a project website
an interactive communication piece

Quarto supports all of these with a common syntax and shared project structure.





Quarto uses readable plain text files with the .qmd extension. That makes documents easier to version, review, and reuse than proprietary binary formats.



A single source file can often render to several outputs such as HTML, PDF, and DOCX. This reduces duplication and keeps content aligned across formats.



Quarto can include executable code chunks in languages such as R, Python, Julia, and Observable JavaScript. That supports reproducible analysis and reporting without forcing everyone into the same language.




A doctoral researcher might use:

one Quarto file as a lab notebook for ongoing observations
another as a manuscript draft that renders to PDF and DOCX
a slide deck generated from the same project for a conference talk
a simple website to share outputs and project updates

That is the larger theme of this workshop: one authoring system, many academic uses.






ImportantQuarto, Markdown, and YAML



Most Quarto files combine three ingredients:

YAML for metadata and configuration
Markdown for the written content
optional code chunks for computation and generated output






Continue to Markdown for Beginners to learn the authoring syntax that most Quarto pages rely on.

title: What Is Quarto?

title: Installing Quarto
text:
Before creating projects, participants need Quarto itself installed on their computer. They also need at least one way to edit plain text files, and optionally a programming environment if they want to run code chunks.


A clear installation path lowers the barrier to entry. Many workshop participants are interested in the publishing workflow first and only later decide how much computation they want to embed.





The Quarto command line interface is the core tool. It handles rendering and project management.



Participants can use:

Positron or RStudio
VS Code
another plain text editor they are comfortable with




If they want executable code chunks, they will also need at least one language runtime such as:

R
Python
Julia






Download Quarto from the official website.
Install it using the standard installer for your operating system.
Open a terminal and run quarto check.
Confirm your editor recognizes .qmd files.
If you want live code execution, confirm that R, Python, or Julia is also available.




A participant who mainly wants to create websites and notes may only need Quarto and an editor at first. A participant building reproducible reports with figures from analysis will also want a runtime such as R or Python installed.






TipWorkshop-friendly setup



For a mixed audience, it helps to frame code execution as optional. Participants can still learn a lot from Quarto even before they start running embedded code.





After installation, participants should be able to answer yes to these questions:

Can I open or create a .qmd file?
Can I run quarto check in a terminal?
Do I know which editor I will use during the workshop?




Once the foundations are in place, move into the first use case: Documenting Research in Progress.

title: Exercise: Build a Short Slide Deck
text:
Create a minimal Quarto presentation that could support a short research talk.


You should know:

basic Markdown headings
how YAML configures document format
that RevealJS is one common Quarto presentation format




You have been asked to give a five-minute update on a project about drought impacts on vegetation. You need a quick slide deck with a title slide, research question, approach, and next steps.




Create a file called talk.qmd.
Add a YAML header with a title and format: revealjs.
Create a first slide with the title and presenter name.
Add slides for research question, methods, key finding, and next steps.
Keep each slide concise using bullets instead of paragraphs.
Add one placeholder line for a future figure.




---
title: "Drought Impacts on Vegetation"
format: revealjs
---

## Research Question

- How did vegetation response differ across sites?

## Methods

- Remote sensing summary
- Field validation



Add speaker notes or a final slide with contact information.



Your slide deck should:

have a clear sequence
be appropriate for a short academic talk
show how Quarto can switch from document writing to presentation authoring




Ask yourself:

Would each slide be readable in a live talk?
Is there one main message per slide?
Can this deck grow into a fuller presentation later?
crumbs: Exercise: Build a Short Slide Deck

title: Exercise: Build a Lab Notebook Page
text:
Create a Quarto document for documenting ongoing research activity in a structured, readable way.


You should be familiar with:

basic Markdown headings and lists
the idea of a YAML header
opening or creating a .qmd file




You are working on a small restoration ecology project. You want a notebook entry that captures this week’s work, next steps, and one optional code area for later use.




Create a new file called notebook.qmd.
Add a YAML header with a title, your name, today’s date, and format: html.
Add a heading for the current week.
Write a short paragraph describing what you did.
Add a bullet list of observations or decisions.
Add a numbered list of next steps.
Add a fenced code block or commented placeholder showing where a future chunk could go.




---
title: "Restoration Project Notebook"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format: html
---

## Week 1

This week I reviewed the sampling plan and checked the latest field observations.

- Confirmed plot IDs
- Flagged missing measurements
- Noted one sensor calibration issue

1. Recheck the field sheet
2. Contact collaborators about the missing values
3. Prepare the next visit



Add a callout block with a reminder about one issue to revisit next week.



By the end, you should have a simple notebook page that:

renders as a readable HTML document
separates narrative, decisions, and tasks clearly
could be reused as a template for future entries




Ask yourself:

Does the page make sense if I read it again in a month?
Can another team member understand what happened this week?
Is the structure simple enough to reuse consistently?
crumbs: Exercise: Build a Lab Notebook Page

title: Templates and Extensions
text:
Once participants are comfortable with Quarto basics, templates and extensions provide ways to speed up repeated work and add specialized functionality.


Academic workflows often repeat familiar patterns:

manuscript structures
recurring workshop materials
shared project websites
journal-specific output requirements

Templates reduce setup time, and extensions can add features that are not included by default.



Templates are useful when you want a consistent starting point for recurring work. A research group might maintain a preferred manuscript skeleton or workshop-page pattern so new documents begin with the right sections already in place.



Quarto extensions can add custom formats, filters, shortcodes, or presentation features. They are installed into a project and become part of its publishing toolkit.
Example install command:
quarto add quarto-ext/fontawesome



A lab could keep:

a manuscript starter structure for new papers
a workshop exercise template
a project website template with agreed navigation and style

Extensions become especially helpful when a project needs journal formats, icons, or specialized presentation behavior.






TipGood habit



Treat extensions like dependencies: document them clearly and only add them when they solve a real need.





For beginners, the key lesson is not memorizing many extensions. It is learning that Quarto can grow with them, from simple documents to more customized publishing workflows.

title: Using brand.yml
text:
brand.yml is a Quarto branding file used to define shared visual identity such as colors, logos, and typography across outputs. It is particularly useful for websites, presentations, and other materials that should feel consistent.


Research groups, projects, and centers often want their outputs to look connected. Instead of styling each document separately, brand.yml provides a shared source of branding choices.



Depending on the project, a branding file may define:

color palette
logo assets
typography choices
reusable visual identity settings




A collaborative project can use one brand configuration so that its website, slide deck, and outreach documents share a recognizable visual style.



In this workshop, brand.yml should be introduced as an advanced organizational tool rather than a first-day requirement. Participants should leave knowing:

what problem it solves
when it becomes useful
why consistent branding can improve professional visibility and project coherence







NoteKeep the priority straight



Branding matters, but structure and clarity matter first. A plain, well-organized Quarto project is more valuable than a beautifully branded one that is hard to maintain.





If this workshop later expands, brand.yml would pair nicely with a follow-up session on custom themes, templates, and project-wide style conventions.

title: Exercise: Plan a Simple Dashboard
text:
Sketch a beginner-friendly Quarto dashboard concept for communicating research results to a non-specialist audience.


You should understand:

how Quarto organizes content into pages
the difference between static and interactive communication
the importance of choosing a clear audience




Your project needs a simple web-based summary for partners who want to monitor three indicators without reading a full report.




Identify the audience for the dashboard.
Choose three indicators or summaries that matter most to them.
Draft a page title and one-sentence purpose statement.
Plan a layout with an introduction, three metric areas, and a short interpretation section.
Write one note about what should stay simple in version one.
Add one sentence explaining where the underlying data would come from.




Use this sentence to begin:

This dashboard helps project partners monitor restoration progress across sites using a small set of regularly updated indicators.




Add an idea for one interactive feature, such as choosing a site or time period.



You should finish with a dashboard concept that:

has a defined audience
avoids trying to show everything at once
could later become a real Quarto dashboard project




Ask yourself:

Is the intended audience clear?
Are the selected indicators genuinely useful?
Does the plan stay focused enough for a first version?

title: Exercise: Draft a Manuscript Skeleton
text:
Create a starter Quarto manuscript structure that is ready for writing, review, and later expansion.


You should be comfortable with:

headings in Markdown
basic YAML metadata
the idea that Quarto can render to more than one format




You are preparing the first draft of a paper on land-use change and pollinator diversity. You want a manuscript skeleton that can later grow into a full article.




Create a new file called manuscript.qmd.
Add a YAML header with title, author, date, and outputs for HTML and DOCX.
Add headings for Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion, and References.
Under each heading, write one or two placeholder sentences.
Add a note where a figure or table will eventually appear.
If you already use citation files, add a placeholder bibliography entry in YAML.




---
title: "Pollinator Diversity Under Land-Use Change"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
bibliography: references.bib
---



Add a subsection under Methods for data collection and another for analysis.



You should end with a manuscript file that:

has a clear academic structure
is ready for collaborative expansion
can later support citations, figures, and tables




Ask yourself:

Does the document already look like the beginning of a paper?
Are the sections in a sensible order for your discipline?
Would a collaborator understand where to add new material?

title: Exercise: Draft a Manuscript Skeleton
text:
You should be comfortable with:

headings in Markdown
basic YAML metadata
the idea that Quarto can render to more than one format

title: Exercise: Sketch a Research Website
text:
Create the skeleton of a small Quarto website for a project or personal academic profile.


You should understand:

the role of _quarto.yml
the difference between a single document and a website project
basic Markdown page authoring




You want a project website for a workshop or research initiative. It should contain a homepage, an about page, and a materials page.




Imagine a new folder for your site project.
Draft an _quarto.yml file with project.type: website.
Add a site title and simple navigation.
Plan three pages: home, about, and materials.
Write one sentence describing the purpose of each page.
Add one callout on the homepage inviting visitors to start with the key materials.




project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "Research Project Site"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About
      - href: materials.qmd
        text: Materials



Add a sidebar or a footer idea that would help visitors navigate the site.



You should have a simple site plan that:

separates page-level content from site-level configuration
reflects a clear audience
could be expanded into a real project website




Ask yourself:

Is the site purpose obvious from the title and planned pages?
Would a visitor know where to click first?
Is the structure small enough to maintain?

title: Markdown for Beginners
text:
Markdown is a lightweight writing syntax. Instead of clicking formatting buttons, you type small symbols directly into plain text. Quarto builds on standard Markdown and adds publishing features that are especially helpful for technical and academic work.


Markdown is worth learning because it is:

fast to write
easy to read in raw form
suitable for version control
flexible across websites, reports, and manuscripts

For workshop participants, Markdown is the main skill that unlocks most of Quarto.





# Main Title
## Section
### Subsection



*italic*
**bold**



- bullet item
- another bullet

1. first step
2. second step



[Quarto](https://quarto.org)
![Figure description](images/field-site.png)



Inline code looks like quarto render.
Code blocks look like this:
```bash
quarto render
```




Quarto keeps standard Markdown, but it also makes room for:

callout blocks for notes, warnings, and tips
cross-references for sections, figures, and tables
executable code chunks
layout features for richer publishing







TipBeginner mindset



You do not need to memorize every Markdown feature at once. For most workshop tasks, headings, lists, links, emphasis, and fenced code blocks are enough to get started.





A methods note written in Quarto might use:

headings to separate data collection, cleaning, and analysis
bullet points for decisions and open questions
links to protocols or repositories
fenced code blocks to show commands used in the workflow




Try writing a short research note with:

a title heading
one short paragraph
a list of three tasks
a link to a project repository or journal website




Continue to YAML Headers to see how Quarto stores document settings at the top of a file.

title: YAML Headers in Quarto
text:
YAML is the metadata block at the top of a Quarto document. It sits between two lines of --- and controls document information such as title, author, date, output formats, and execution settings.


YAML is where Quarto moves from “plain text note” to “configured publication”. A well-chosen header can make the same source file render as a report, manuscript, slide deck, or website page.



---
title: "Fieldwork Notes"
author: "A. Researcher"
date: today
format: html
---





This includes the title, subtitle, authors, date, and sometimes affiliations or keywords.



This tells Quarto what to create, such as HTML, PDF, DOCX, or revealjs slides.



This controls how code chunks behave across the file, for example whether code is shown or whether warnings are hidden.




A manuscript draft might use a header like this:
---
title: "How Soil Moisture Shapes Restoration Outcomes"
author:
  - "Research Team"
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
  pdf: default
bibliography: references.bib
---
That one header already signals a multi-format workflow.






WarningCommon beginner issue



YAML is sensitive to indentation. If spacing is inconsistent, rendering can fail even when the content looks almost correct.






YAML configures the document
Markdown writes the document
code chunks generate results inside the document

Keeping these roles separate makes Quarto much easier to understand.



Continue to Installing Quarto for a practical overview of what participants need on their machine.

date: today
format: html
---

title: 1 AI assistance
text:
1 AI assistance
Add the quarto-authoring skill for IDE agents from: https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/quarto-authoring-migration

title: Documenting Research in Progress
text:
Quarto works well as a lab notebook or research log because it combines readable text, structured metadata, and optional code in one plain text document. That makes it a strong fit for exploratory work that changes over time.


Research in progress generates many small but important pieces of context:

what you tried
what worked and failed
which files or datasets you used
what to do next

A Quarto notebook helps capture that context in a durable and shareable format.





Markdown headings make it easy to separate entries by date, experiment, or task.



YAML can record authorship, date, project name, and output preferences.



If you are using R or Python, you can place data summaries or quick visual checks directly beside your notes.



An HTML version may be useful for browsing, while PDF can work for archiving or sharing.




Imagine a field ecology project. A researcher can keep one .qmd file for weekly notes that includes:

observations from the field
links to raw data files
a short list of unresolved issues
a simple chunk that prints the number of new observations collected







NoteGood notebook habit



Write what you decided and why, not only what you did. Future-you will care about the reasoning.





The exercise page walks through creating a starter research notebook with headings, tasks, and a simple output format.
Go to the lab notebook exercise

title: Creating Presentations
text:
Quarto can create slide decks using formats such as RevealJS. This is useful for researchers who want presentations to live close to the same material as their reports, papers, and websites.


Talks often reuse the same ideas, figures, and summaries that also appear in manuscripts or project documentation. A shared authoring workflow helps reduce duplication and keeps messaging consistent.





In a Quarto slide deck, level-two headings usually create new slides.



Speaker notes, figures, and short summaries can be adapted from existing Quarto material.



HTML slides are easy to share and update, and they work well for teaching or conference talks.




A conference presentation can be produced from the same project that contains the paper draft and supporting notes. That makes it easier to keep claims, numbers, and figures aligned.






NotePresentation mindset



A good slide deck is not a full paper on screen. Quarto makes it easy to write too much, so concise content still matters.





The exercise page shows how to build a small presentation skeleton for an academic audience.
Go to the presentations exercise
